comments.title: Intro says pruning applies to all weights, but later setup limits the prunable scope
comments.quote: Starting with the pre-trained model, we prune all weights whose magnitude is below a threshold chosen in order to achieve a target number of FLOPs for the pruned model;
comments.explanation: I initially read this as defining global magnitude pruning over every trainable weight in the FBNetV3 model. I checked the later setup to see whether this was just introductory shorthand. Section 3.1 says the experiments are “mostly interested in 1 _×_ 1 convolutions as potentially prunable,” because those layers dominate FLOPs and because the sparse kernel support used for latency benchmarking is available only for fully connected layers after transforming 1 _×_ 1 convolutions. That later context explains the practical choice, but it leaves the introductory method description materially inaccurate: “all weights” and, in the uniform case, “weights in each layer” suggest a different pruning domain than the one apparently used for the reported FLOP targets. This matters for reproducibility and for interpreting the sparsity/FLOP tradeoff, because pruning depthwise convolutions, final classifiers, or other layers would lead to different thresholds, sparsity distributions, and possibly accuracy/latency outcomes. A more precise statement would restrict the pruning to the selected prunable layers, e.g. the 1 _×_ 1 convolution weights.

comments.title: FLOP equality for unstructured sparse models relies on an implementation assumption
comments.quote: Can a model obtained by pruning a larger FBNetV3 model **M1** (optimized using NAS) achieve higher generalization performance than a smaller FBNetV3 model **M2** when the pruned model has the same number of FLOPs as **M2** ?
comments.explanation: The research question is understandable: the authors want a clean FLOPs–accuracy comparison between a pruned large model and a smaller dense FBNetV3 model. Later context also shows that they are aware of the hardware issue and include latency benchmarking with sparse kernels. Still, in unstructured pruning, merely setting weights to zero does not reduce the operations executed by ordinary dense convolution kernels; the reduced FLOP count is an idealized sparse arithmetic count that assumes zero weights are skipped efficiently. Because the abstract and title frame the work as efficient inference, a careful reader could incorrectly infer that equal nominal FLOPs immediately implies comparable inference cost to the smaller dense model. The passage would be clearer if it explicitly stated that the FLOP accounting assumes sparse execution support, while latency/real hardware speedups are evaluated separately.

comments.title: State-of-the-art conclusion is broader than the stated comparison establishes
comments.quote: The resulting family of pruned models can consistently obtain _better_ performance than existing FBNetV3 models at the same level of computation, and thus provide state-of-the-art results when trading off between computational complexity and generalization performance on the ImageNet benchmark.
comments.explanation: I understand the intended argument: FBNetV3 is presented as a strong NAS-derived efficient ConvNet family, so improving on its FLOPs–accuracy curve is a meaningful result. The surrounding context does support treating FBNetV3 as an important state-of-the-art baseline. However, the phrase “and thus” is too strong as written. Outperforming existing FBNetV3 variants at matched nominal FLOPs does not by itself establish state-of-the-art results across all relevant efficient ImageNet models, pruning methods, quantized models, or latency-optimized architectures. This is especially time-sensitive given that “state-of-the-art” changes rapidly; as of the current date, the claim would need either temporal qualification or comprehensive contemporary benchmarking. The claim would be safer if limited to “improves over the FBNetV3 FLOPs–accuracy curve” unless the full experimental section provides broader comparisons.

comments.title: “First of its kind” novelty claim is overbroad given acknowledged prior work
comments.quote: This paper is the first of its kind to understand how we can improve on the state-of-the-art in this problem space.
comments.explanation: The intended novelty seems to be narrower and plausible: studying pruning specifically for FBNetV3/NARS-derived compact ConvNets and comparing the compute cost of pruning against running NAS. I checked the surrounding context, and the authors themselves acknowledge prior pruning work on efficient convolutional models such as EfficientNet and MobileNet, as well as joint prune-and-architecture search. That context resolves part of the concern, but the quoted wording remains broader than justified. Since related work in compact-model pruning already exists, “first of its kind” should be narrowed to the specific setting—e.g. pruning FBNetV3 models, improving the FBNetV3 FLOPs–accuracy frontier, or evaluating pruning as a lower-GPU-hour alternative to FBNetV3-style search.

comments.title: Deployment/latency claim is broader than the later sparse-kernel setup supports
comments.quote: These results augment our main observations and demonstrate that with existing hardware support, it is possible to deploy pruned cutting-edge computer vision models with practical latency reductions and improve further beyond the performance vs. FLOPs trade-off.
comments.explanation: I initially read this as claiming that the main pruned FBNetV3 models studied in the paper can be deployed with practical latency reductions using existing sparse-kernel support. The surrounding paragraph is an overview, so some forward-looking simplification is expected. However, checking the later methodology resolves this only partially: Section 3.3 says the latency experiments use uniform layer-wise block-based pruning with block size \(1 \times 4\), int8 quantization, and PyTorch sparse quantized linear kernels on Intel Skylake CPUs, while the main pruning methods are described as unstructured magnitude pruning of \(1 \times 1\) convolutions. The authors also later acknowledge that current hardware is not well suited for unstructured random sparsity. Thus the broad statement about deploying 'pruned cutting-edge computer vision models' with practical latency reductions could mislead readers into thinking the FLOPs/accuracy pruned models directly translate to latency gains on existing hardware. The claim would be stronger if it explicitly noted that the demonstrated latency reductions require a specific block-sparse, quantized CPU-kernel setting and may not apply to the unstructured sparse models used for the main accuracy/FLOPs comparisons.

comments.title: Same-FLOPs claim may conflate theoretical sparse FLOPs with realized inference cost
comments.quote: We show that computationally less intensive techniques such as uniform and global magnitude-based pruning of larger FBNetV3 models can yield higher test accuracies than small models while having the same number of FLOPs.
comments.explanation: The intended comparison is clear: prune a larger FBNetV3 model until its counted FLOPs match a smaller FBNetV3 model, then compare accuracy. That is a reasonable experimental question, and later sections explain that only \(1 \times 1\) convolutions are pruned because they dominate the FLOPs. What remains potentially misleading is the phrase 'same number of FLOPs' for globally/uniformly magnitude-pruned unstructured models. In standard dense inference, zeroing individual weights does not automatically reduce actual executed multiply-adds unless the implementation uses sparse kernels; realized compute depends strongly on sparse format, kernel support, block structure, and hardware. The paper later separates the latency benchmark and uses block-sparse quantized kernels, which are not exactly the same as the main unstructured pruning setup. A careful reader can infer that the authors are using a theoretical nonzero-operation FLOP count, but the passage does not say this. Since the paper’s central comparison is a FLOPs-accuracy curve, it would help to qualify that the 'same FLOPs' statement refers to counted sparse FLOPs/nonzero operations under an assumed sparse execution model, not necessarily dense-kernel runtime cost.

comments.title: GPU-hour comparison depends on the assumption that a larger NAS model is already available
comments.quote: Pruning a publicly available NAS optimized model incurs _≈_ 4x less GPU hours to achieve a target FLOPs level, compared to training a full-fledged NAS to obtain a model which has less accuracy at the same FLOPs level.
comments.explanation: The authors are addressing RQ2, and the context does state the intended practical scenario: a practitioner already has access to a publicly released larger FBNetV3 model and wants a cheaper way to reach a target FLOP budget. Under that assumption, comparing pruning/fine-tuning cost against running a new NAS is sensible. The remaining concern is that the wording could be read more generally as saying pruning is intrinsically 4x cheaper than NAS for obtaining such models, without accounting for the sunk cost of producing the starting NAS-optimized model. The phrase 'publicly available NAS optimized model' partly resolves this, but because the sentence is in the contribution summary, the comparison should be explicitly scoped to the reuse-of-existing-model setting. Otherwise the GPU-hour advantage is not an apples-to-apples end-to-end model development cost; it is a marginal adaptation cost.

comments.title: ResNet discussion blurs optimization benefits with inference-efficiency benefits
comments.quote: This can be performed either through the design of new simplified layers, for example in deep residual learning (He et al., 2016) or though explicit model compression as in weight quantization (Polino et al., 2018). Extremely deep networks for image recognition often suffer from not only high complexity and inference latency, but also from the issue of _vanishing gradients_ (Pascanu et al., 2013). This was addressed through deep residual networks which effectively simplified network design through skip-connections.
comments.explanation: I understand the authors’ broader point: architectural design can improve practical neural-network performance, and residual connections are a foundational architectural innovation for vision models. The following sentence also correctly notes that residual connections address optimization difficulties such as vanishing/degraded gradients in very deep networks. What remains confusing is that this paragraph is specifically about computationally efficient vision models, and the first sentence presents deep residual learning as an example of 'new simplified layers' for efficiency. Standard ResNets are primarily an optimization/depth-enabling architecture, not a model-compression or low-latency technique; skip connections often enable deeper networks and do not by themselves reduce FLOPs or memory footprint. The wording could lead readers to conflate improved trainability with improved inference efficiency. This is a localized related-work framing issue, not a problem with the paper’s pruning methodology.

comments.title: Sparse-layer FLOP formula uses sparsity as if it were density
comments.quote:
The number of FLOPs consumed by a linear layer of sparsity _S_ is proportional to the number of sparse matrix multiplications performed and is given by _S ∗ F_ , where _F_ is the corresponding dense FLOPs. Thus if _F_ 1 _×_ 1( _X_ ) is the number of FLOPs consumed by the 1 _×_ 1 convolution layers and _F_ ( _x_ ) is the total number of FLOPs consumed by model _X_ , we have:

_S_ = ( _F_ ( _X_ ) _− F_ ( _Y_ )) _/F_ 1 _×_ 1( _X_ )
comments.explanation: The intended setup is clear: only the 1×1 convolution FLOPs of the larger model X are reduced, and S is later described as the fraction of 1×1 weights removed. Under that interpretation, Eq. (1) is the natural derivation: the pruned model has FLOPs F(X) - S F_{1×1}(X), and setting this equal to F(Y) gives S = (F(X)-F(Y))/F_{1×1}(X). What remains problematic is the preceding sentence, which says the FLOPs consumed by a layer of sparsity S are S * F. If S is the fraction removed, the consumed FLOPs should be approximately (1-S)F, while S F is the FLOP reduction. This is not just a wording issue, because it directly contradicts the subsequent definition of sparsity and the derivation of Eq. (1).

comments.title: Use of “sparsity level” is ambiguous for global pruning and FLOP matching
comments.quote: For each initial and target FBNetV3 model _X_ and _Y_ , where _X_ is larger than _Y_ , we prune _X_ to a _sparsity level_ of _S_ so that the FLOP count is the same as for _Y_ .
comments.explanation: I initially read S as a single scalar sparsity applied to all prunable 1×1 convolution layers, which is consistent with the subsequent uniform-pruning equation. The later paragraph partially resolves this by saying Eq. (1) is for uniform pruning and that global pruning target sparsities are computed empirically from layer shapes. However, this opening sentence still introduces S as if there is a single sparsity level that uniquely determines the FLOP count for both pruning methods. For global magnitude pruning, layer sparsities can differ substantially, and an unweighted parameter sparsity does not generally map directly to a target FLOP reduction because different 1×1 layers operate at different spatial resolutions. The paper seems aware of this, but the notation here could mislead readers unless S is explicitly qualified as a FLOP-weighted sparsity, a uniform per-layer sparsity, or only a shorthand for the achieved pruning target.

comments.title: Unstructured pruning is described as pruning neurons rather than weights/connections
comments.quote: Unstructured pruning, as the name suggests, doesn’t adhere to any structure and prunes neurons based on chosen criteria (such as magnitude).
comments.explanation: The authors’ intended contrast between unstructured and structured pruning is standard and mostly clear from the surrounding discussion. However, in standard terminology unstructured pruning removes individual weights/connections, not neurons; pruning neurons, channels, filters, or blocks is normally considered structured pruning because it removes whole computational units. This matters slightly here because the paper’s own method is weight-magnitude pruning of 1×1 convolution weights, and the hardware discussion depends on the distinction between irregular weight sparsity and structured sparsity. A knowledgeable reader can infer the intended meaning, but the sentence is technically inaccurate.

comments.title: Fine-tuning and early-stopping details are underspecified for reproducibility and GPU-hour comparisons
comments.quote: We have conducted a hyper-parameter tuning for the learning rate parameter, with LR values in the set {4e-5, 8e-5, 1.6e-4}, as fine-tuning generally admits smaller learning rates than training from scratch. We have found that using the same learning rate for all models, along with the same hyper-parameter settings used for training the seed model is sufficient to obtain pruned networks which are superior to the baseline FBNetV3 models. Hence minimal hyper-parameter tuning was required for our experiments and we have used values of settings such as weight decay and momentum to be the same as those used for training the baseline FBNetV3 models. During fine-tuning after pruning, we have used a smoothed validation loss to stop the process early after a convergence tolerance (0.01%) is reached between two consecutive epochs. Generally, we have observed fine-tuning to converge around _∼_ 250 epochs.
comments.explanation: The authors do provide useful high-level information: the LR search set, the hardware, the fact that the same settings were reused across models, and an approximate convergence time. I checked whether this might be enough because FBNetV3 recipes may be available externally, but several choices that affect both final accuracy and the claimed GPU-hour savings remain implicit. The selected learning rate is not stated; the optimizer, schedule, batch size, augmentation/regularization details, and exact inherited recipe are not specified here; and the smoothed validation-loss stopping rule does not define the smoothing window or whether the 0.01% tolerance is relative or absolute. Since one of the paper’s central claims is that pruning is computationally cheaper than NAS, the stopping criterion and fine-tuning recipe are not merely implementation trivia—they determine the reported compute cost and final accuracy.

comments.title: Reported “overall model sparsity” conflicts with the paper’s earlier sparsity definition
comments.quote: We measure overall model sparsity, which is different to the layer sparsity since we only prune 1 _×_ 1 convolution layers, as explained in Section 3.1.
comments.explanation: I initially read this as saying that the sparsity values reported in the metrics section are aggregate model sparsities, i.e. zeros divided by all weights in the whole network, including unpruned layers. But Section 3.1 defines the sparsity level \(S\) used for the pruning target as the fraction of the \(1\times1\) convolution weights/FLOPs removed, and Table 1’s uniform-pruning sparsity values appear to be those target \(1\times1\)-layer sparsities from Eq. (1), not whole-model sparsities. If only \(1\times1\) convolutions are pruned, the overall model sparsity would generally be lower than the sparsity within the pruned layers. The authors are right to note that layer-wise sparsity and aggregate sparsity differ, but the passage leaves unclear which quantity is actually reported and compared in Table 1.

comments.title: Latency benchmarking procedure is underspecified for reproduction
comments.quote: Prior to using these kernels, we perform uniform layer-wise block-based pruning with block sizes of 1 _×_ 4. Magnitude based pruning is implemented at block level, and the model is quantized to 8-bit integers (int8) before latency benchmarking, which is performed on Intel CPUs designed using the Skylake micro-architecture.
comments.explanation: The high-level intent is clear: because unstructured sparsity is hard to accelerate, the authors switch to block sparsity and int8 sparse kernels for latency measurements. However, several choices that materially affect latency and accuracy are left implicit. For block pruning, the paper does not specify how a block’s magnitude is computed, e.g. sum, mean, max, or norm of the weights; how thresholds are chosen to match target FLOPs; whether the same fine-tuning protocol is reused after block pruning and quantization; and which layers are converted to sparse quantized linear operators. For latency, standard reporting usually includes batch size, input resolution, number of CPU threads, whether MKL-DNN/FBGEMM settings are fixed, warmup/measurement iterations, whether reshape overheads are included, and whether the dense baseline is quantized and benchmarked under identical conditions. Without these details, the latency results are difficult to reproduce or interpret quantitatively.

comments.title: One Table 1 gain value is numerically inconsistent with its baseline and accuracy
comments.explanation: Most entries in the Gain(%) columns appear to be relative percentage improvements over the baseline accuracy, not absolute percentage-point gains. For example, \((80.738/79.6 - 1)\times100 \approx 1.43\), matching the C→A uniform-pruning row. Applying the same convention to the quoted D→C global-pruning row gives \((81.208/80.8 - 1)\times100 \approx 0.505\%\), not 1.256%. The reported 1.256% would be consistent with comparing 81.208 against approximately 80.2, i.e. the FBNetV3B baseline, not the target FBNetV3C baseline shown in the row. This looks like a localized table error rather than a conceptual flaw, but it affects one of the reported gains.

comments.title: Caption says the best accuracy per FLOP level is bolded, but the table bolding appears method-specific
comments.quote: Table 1: Sparsity level (in percentage) and performance of pruned FBNetV3 networks on ImageNet dataset for different target MFLOPs. The best accuracy obtained at each target FLOPs level is highlighted in bold.
comments.explanation: I first interpreted this caption literally: for each target MFLOPs value, exactly the highest Top-1 accuracy among all rows and pruning methods at that target should be bolded. But the table often bolds separate uniform-pruning and global-pruning entries at the same target level. For example, at 356.6 MFLOPs, both the uniform and global C→A accuracies are bolded, even though 80.738 is higher than 80.476. Similarly, at 557.0 MFLOPs, E→C uniform has the highest listed accuracy, while D→C global is also bolded. This is likely intended to mark the best result within each pruning method at a target FLOP level, which is a reasonable convention, but the caption does not say that and can mislead a reader scanning the table.

comments.title: Results text says Table 1 includes parameter counts, but the shown table does not
comments.quote: Table 1 presents the accuracy and number of parameters of the pruned models at each target FLOPs level.
comments.explanation: The surrounding discussion is about accuracy/FLOPs tradeoffs, and Table 1 indeed reports target MFLOPs, baseline accuracy, sparsity, Top-1 accuracy, and gain. However, it does not contain a number-of-parameters column in the provided table. This may be an editing artifact or a remnant from an earlier version of the table, but as written it promises a metric that is not actually shown. Since parameter count is one of the stated metrics in Section 3.3, the absence is mildly confusing.

comments.title: GPU-hour comparison omits key accounting details needed to support the RQ2 cost claim
comments.quote: In Table 2, for each of the best performing uniformly-pruned models in Section 4.1 we report the number of GPU-hours consumed by the prune and fine-tune strategy, along with the GPU-hours consumed when obtaining a FBNetV3 model through architecture search using the method described in Dai et al. (2020).
comments.explanation: The authors do state the intended scenario: a pretrained larger FBNetV3 seed model is already available, so pruning can be cheaper for a practitioner than running NAS from scratch. That context resolves the strongest fairness objection. What remains underspecified is the accounting behind the reported speedup. It is unclear whether the pruning GPU-hours include only the final fine-tuning run or also the learning-rate sweep, unsuccessful seed/target trials, mask construction, quantization/block-pruning experiments, and selection of the best uniformly pruned model. Conversely, the NAS cost is a constant 10.7k GPU-hours for several target models, but the text does not explain whether this includes architecture search only, search plus final training, recipe search, or amortized search over the FBNetV3 family. Because RQ2 is a central claim, these accounting choices should be explicit to make the comparison reproducible and fair under the stated pretrained-seed assumption.

comments.title: Claim of improving the state of the art is stronger than the evidence shown in this passage
comments.quote: This clearly shows that pruning FBNetV3 models with minimal fine-tuning can significantly improve on the state-of-the-art for FLOPs vs. Top-1 accuracy trade-off.
comments.explanation: The table and figure do support a narrower and interesting claim: pruned larger FBNetV3 models often outperform smaller FBNetV3 baselines at matched FLOPs. Since FBNetV3 is presented earlier as a strong NAS-optimized family, this is meaningful evidence. However, the quoted sentence frames the result as an improvement on the state of the art for the FLOPs/Top-1 tradeoff in general. In the provided passage, Figure 1 compares against the existing FBNetV3 family, not against the full landscape of contemporary efficient ConvNets, training recipes, resolutions, distillation/augmentation variants, or sparse/quantized deployment settings. The wording would be safer if it said the method improves over the FBNetV3 FLOPs–accuracy curve, unless broader comparisons are provided elsewhere.

comments.title: FLOPs are treated as capturing power consumption too directly
comments.quote: We report the model FLOPs, because this metric captures the computational footprint of the model and its power consumption.
comments.explanation: Reporting FLOPs is standard and useful for comparing computational complexity, especially in architecture-search papers. The concern is the second part of the sentence: FLOPs do not directly capture power consumption, particularly for sparse models, where memory access patterns, indexing overhead, quantization, cache behavior, batch size, and hardware kernel support often dominate realized latency and energy. The authors themselves acknowledge later in the same section that sparsity-to-speedup translation is hardware-dependent. This is therefore best framed as FLOPs being a proxy or correlate for computational cost, not as a metric that captures power consumption.

comments.title: Accuracy–latency curve does not clearly specify whether accuracy is measured on the quantized sparse model
comments.quote: We have pruned all 1 _×_ 1 convolution layers uniformly here and subsequently converted them to fully-connected layers for compatibility with the quantized sparse kernels. In Figure 2a, we present the Top-1 ImageNet accuracy vs. latency curve
comments.explanation: I understand the authors’ intent: Section 3.3 says the latency experiment uses 1 _×_ 4 block sparsity, converts 1 _×_ 1 convolutions to linear layers, and benchmarks with int8 quantized sparse kernels. That explains why the conversion is needed. What remains unclear is whether the Top-1 accuracy in the plotted latency–accuracy curve is measured on exactly the same int8, block-sparse, converted model whose latency is benchmarked, or whether accuracy is measured on a floating-point pruned model while latency is measured after quantization. This matters because quantization can change ImageNet accuracy, and the effect can interact with high sparsity. The passage also does not state key latency-benchmarking choices such as batch size, thread count, number of warmup/runs, or whether reshape/conversion overhead is included end-to-end. Some of this may be in an appendix, but in the provided method description the latency-performance tradeoff is not fully reproducible and could be interpreted as combining metrics from non-identical model variants.

comments.title: Layer sensitivity experiment is underspecified with respect to fine-tuning and evaluation protocol
comments.quote: For analysis (2) we prune each 1 _×_ 1 convolution layer in isolation at a sparsity target of 95% and record the Top-1 test accuracy obtained on ImageNet dataset.
comments.explanation: The intended analysis is clear and useful: prune one layer at a time to identify which layer types are fragile under extreme sparsity. The surrounding sentence says this is done under uniform layer-wise magnitude pruning, so the pruning criterion is mostly resolved by context. However, the procedure is still missing details that materially affect the interpretation. It is not stated whether the network is evaluated immediately after pruning, whether batch-normalization statistics are recalibrated, or whether any fine-tuning is performed after pruning each isolated layer. These alternatives answer different questions: immediate evaluation measures local damage from the mask, while fine-tuned evaluation measures recoverability. At 95% sparsity, this distinction can be very large. Also, the use of “test accuracy” is potentially misleading in the ImageNet setting, where most papers report validation accuracy and repeated ablations should not generally be tuned on a held-out test set. The conclusion about PW/PWL/SE sensitivity would be easier to assess if these protocol details were explicit.

comments.title: Global sparsity patterns are interpreted more causally than the evidence supports
comments.quote: We note that the sparsity level in lower layers is lower which is potentially required for maintaining the performance . Higher sparsity can be admitted in upper layers of the network where it has learnt more redundant representations.
comments.explanation: The observation itself is reasonable: Figure 2b apparently shows that global magnitude pruning leaves lower layers denser and prunes some upper layers more aggressively. The authors also use cautious wording such as “potentially,” and the later isolated-layer analysis partly supports the idea that some layer types are less sensitive. Still, a global magnitude pruning pattern is not, by itself, direct evidence that lower-layer density is required for accuracy or that upper layers have learned more redundant representations. Global magnitude masks are confounded by layer-wise scale differences, normalization, weight decay, activation statistics, and layer shapes; a layer can receive high sparsity because its weights have smaller magnitudes, not necessarily because it is less functionally important. The statement would be better framed as a hypothesis suggested by the pruning pattern, unless supported by controlled layer-wise ablations at comparable FLOP/parameter removal.

comments.title: Near-complete SE weight pruning does not by itself justify claims about removing SE layers or latency gains
comments.quote: SE (Squeeze and Excitation) 1 _×_ 1 convolution layers generally tend to get pruned more compared to other layers, with the sparsity being >99% for two such SE layers in stage _xif_ 5_0. This indicates that we can also consider revisiting SE layer role in FBNetV3 networks, and even remove entire layers in future work to yield additional latency and FLOPs benefits.
comments.explanation: The authors are careful to present this as future work, and the empirical observation that some SE 1 _×_ 1 layers become more than 99% sparse is interesting. However, the inference from very high unstructured sparsity in SE weights to possible removal of entire SE layers is not fully supported by the evidence shown here. An SE module performs a structured gating operation; removing the whole module changes the computation differently from retaining a few sparse weights, and the remaining nonzero weights may still be functionally important. In addition, SE layers often contribute relatively little to total FLOPs and latency compared with pointwise convolutions, so the expected “latency and FLOPs benefits” from removing them may be modest unless quantified. The claim is plausible as a motivation, but it should be phrased more cautiously or backed by an actual SE-removal ablation.

comments.title: Conclusion ambiguously suggests pruning improvements for FBNetV3A itself, although the setup compares larger seeds to smaller targets
comments.quote: This happens not only for relatively over-parameterized networks such as FBNetV3G, but also smaller networks such as FBNetV3A which have lower computational complexity.
comments.explanation: I can see a likely intended meaning: the authors may mean that the improvement is observed even at low FLOP targets, for example when a larger FBNetV3 model is pruned down to the FBNetV3A computation level and compared against dense FBNetV3A. That would be consistent with the paper’s RQ1 setup, where a larger model _M1_ is pruned to match a smaller target _M2_. As written, however, the sentence sounds like FBNetV3A itself is one of the pruned seed networks for which the same-FLOPs improvement is demonstrated. Since FBNetV3A is described as a smaller member of the family, and the setup requires an initial model _X_ larger than target _Y_, this is potentially misleading unless there is an even smaller FBNetV3 target not described here. The wording should distinguish “small FLOP targets such as FBNetV3A” from “small seed networks such as FBNetV3A.”
